title: Making a wove papermaking mould
subtitle: Building the frame and mesh for Western hand papermaking
excerpt: A wove mould produces sheets without the laid-wire pattern visible in traditional chain-line paper. The construction is a straightforward frame-and-mesh build: cut and join four timber strips, stretch a polyester mesh over the frame, and staple it taut. A matching deckle completes the set.
sourceNotes: Wove mould construction documented in Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), Internet Archive: public domain. Hunter describes both wove and laid moulds in detail, including the transition to wove mesh in Western papermaking from approximately the mid-18th century onward, when James Whatman introduced the wove weave at his Kent mill.

glossaryTerms.definition: A papermaking mould covered with a finely woven mesh that leaves no wire-line pattern in the finished sheet. Produces a smooth, even surface when held to light, unlike the laid-and-chain-line pattern of a laid mould.

The removable frame that sits on top of the mould during sheet forming, defining the edges of the sheet. Removed after the pull; the deckle edge of handmade paper is the soft, feathered margin left where pulp crept under the frame.

A papermaking mould covered with closely spaced parallel wires (laid wires) crossed by spaced chain wires. The laid wires leave a characteristic parallel-line shadow in the finished sheet visible when it is held to light.

The orientation of the wood's long fibres relative to the cut. For a papermaking mould, the grain should run along the length of each strip so the frame resists bowing when repeatedly wetted and dried.

content.text: Western papermaking moulds come in two types: laid and
wove

. The laid mould leaves a pattern of parallel wire shadows in the sheet, visible when held to light. The wove mould leaves none, because its covering is a plain weave mesh rather than a wire grid. The
laid mould

is harder to build at home: it requires wire-laying jigs and soldering. The wove mould can be built with basic woodworking tools and a roll of polyester mesh. This tutorial builds one wove mould and the matching
deckle
for A4 sheet forming.

attrs.title: Cutting tools
attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones: a blunt blade skids. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut against a metal ruler held firmly with the non-cutting hand at least two finger-widths back from the blade's path. Cut away from the body. Use a self-healing cutting mat.

items.name: Softwood or hardwood strip, 15 mm wide x 6 mm deep, kiln-dried, straight grain
items.qty: Approximately 1.6 m for an A4 mould plus deckle
items.substitutions: Ramin dowel strip, pine batten, or birch strip all work. The wood must be dry and straight-grained: wet or warped timber will distort on the first papermaking session.

items.name: Fine mesh screen, 40-60 mesh woven polyester or nylon
items.qty: One piece slightly larger than the A4 frame
items.substitutions: Silk screen printing mesh (40-60 threads per centimetre) gives an excellent surface. Avoid metal mesh: it corrodes when wet repeatedly.

items.name: Waterproof wood glue
items.substitutions: Any waterproof PVA wood glue. Standard PVA (non-waterproof) will fail when the mould is repeatedly dipped in water.

items.name: Copper or brass staples, 6 mm leg
items.substitutions: Copper staples are best: they do not rust. Standard galvanised staples rust quickly in a wet environment and can stain the paper. Use a heavy-duty staple gun.

content.text: Cutting and preparing the timber

For an A4 mould (210 x 297 mm sheet size), cut four timber strips for the mould frame and four matching strips for the deckle. The outer dimensions of each frame should be the sheet size plus twice the frame width on each side. With 15 mm-wide timber, the outer dimensions of both the mould and deckle frames are 240 x 327 mm.
Cut two long strips at 327 mm and two short strips at 210 mm for each frame. Check the
grain direction

before cutting: the grain should run along the length of each strip. Sand all cut ends smooth with 240-grit sandpaper and sand the top face of the mould frame, which will contact the mesh, to remove any roughness that could snag the fibres.
Joining the corners

The simplest joint for a home-built mould is a butt joint: the short strips butt up against the inside faces of the long strips. Apply a thin line of waterproof wood glue to the end of each short strip. Hold the joint square and push a staple through the long strip into the short strip end from the outside face to reinforce the glue joint while it sets. Clamp if possible; if not, hold the frame square on a flat surface and place a weight on it while the glue sets. Check for square by measuring diagonally corner to corner: both diagonals must be equal. Allow the glue to cure fully (24 hours at room temperature) before stretching the mesh.
Stretching and stapling the mesh

Cut a piece of polyester mesh approximately 30 mm larger than the frame on all sides. Lay the frame face-down on the mesh. The face of the mould (the side that will contact the pulp) faces down; the underside faces up. Starting at the centre of one long side, fold the mesh over the frame edge and place two or three staples in the centre. Work outward from the centre to the corners on that side, pulling the mesh just taut as you go. Do not pull so hard that you distort the weave.

Move to the opposite long side and repeat: start at the centre, pull the mesh firmly across the frame (not so tight that the frame bows), and staple from the centre outward. Then do the two short sides in the same way. The mesh should be even and under consistent tension when you are done. Trim the excess mesh close to the staple line with a craft knife.
Building the deckle

The deckle is an identical frame with no mesh. Cut and join the strips to exactly the same outer dimensions as the mould frame. The deckle sits on top of the mould during the pull, with its inner opening aligned with the mould's mesh. Sand the lower face of the deckle smooth so it beds squarely onto the mould without gaps. A good deckle-to-mould fit reduces pulp seeping under the frame edge, which causes the characteristic thick, feathered deckle edge of handmade paper.
Finishing and care

Before using the mould for the first time, submerge it in water for a few minutes and check that the corners do not open and the mesh does not slacken. A properly glued butt joint withstands repeated wetting. After each papermaking session, rinse both mould and deckle in clean water, remove any clinging fibre, and stand them upright to dry. Do not lay them flat while wet: the wet mesh distorts under its own weight over time. Store in a dry place. A well-built mould lasts for hundreds of sessions.
Variations

The same construction method scales to any sheet size: A5 for postcard-format sheets, A3 for larger work. A larger mould needs a central support bar across the width to prevent the long sides bowing when the mould is lifted full of water. For the transition from wove to
laid mould
construction, the process of laying chain wires over laid wires across the frame is a more involved project requiring fine brass wire and a soldering setup.
